title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to a Facebook video by David Barton in which Barton uses Exodus, Deuteronomy, a football analogy, an alleged 1843 John Quincy Adams bill, and Ecclesiastes 11 to argue for incrementalism over immediatism in the abortion fight. Silberman identifies four problems: a misunderstanding of immediatism, a flawed first-down analogy, ahistorical claims about abolitionists delaying slavery's end, and the reality that incrementalists are the ones blocking strategic plurality.
audiences: pro-life Christian considering the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate
Texas Republican delegate or activist evaluating party platform priorities
history-minded reader fact-checking David Barton's claims about John Quincy Adams
state legislator or staffer weighing abolition bills against incremental pro-life bills
abolitionist debater preparing rebuttals to common incrementalist arguments

questions.question: What was the immediate occasion for David Barton's video, and why does Silberman think it was aimed at Texas abolitionists?
questions.audience: Texas Republican delegate or activist evaluating party platform priorities
questions.answer: Barton, the former Vice Chair of the Texas Republican Party, posted the video the week of the Texas GOP state convention, where Texas abolitionists were pushing to make immediate abolition of abortion an official legislative priority of the party.
questions.quote: Barton is the former Vice Chair of the Texas Republican Party, so the video was likely in response to Texas abolitionists who are seeking to make abortion's immediate abolition an official legislative priority of the Texas GOP at this week's state party convention.

questions.question: Does immediatism mean that abortion will be abolished overnight?
questions.audience: pro-life Christian considering the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: No. Silberman insists immediatism is not 'overnight-ism' and that immediatists, like their 19th-century predecessors, expect a long struggle. The disagreement is about what the demand should be, not about how quickly victory will arrive.
questions.quote: Immediatism is not overnight-ism. Immediatists are not under the impression that the culture will drastically change overnight or that the object of their protest will be abolished overnight.

questions.question: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to show that early abolitionists distinguished between demand and timeline?
questions.audience: abolitionist debater preparing rebuttals to common incrementalist arguments
questions.answer: He cites Garrison's August 13, 1831 Liberator essay, in which Garrison concedes that abolition will in fact be gradual in execution while still insisting the demand must be for immediate abolition.
questions.quote: As William Lloyd Garrison wrote in the August 13, 1831 edition of *The Liberator*, "Urge immediate abolition as earnestly as we may, it will alas! be gradual abolition in the end. We have never said that slavery would be overthrown by a single blow; that it ought to be we shall always contend."

questions.question: Why does Silberman say incremental bills like 20-week bans or heartbeat bills cannot be grounded in Scripture or the Constitution?
questions.audience: pro-life Christian considering the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: Because such bills concede legality of abortion before some arbitrary developmental threshold, they implicitly deny equal personhood to younger preborn children, violating both the Sixth Commandment and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause.
questions.quote: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

questions.question: What is wrong with Barton's football analogy comparing incremental bills to first downs?
questions.audience: state legislator or staffer weighing abolition bills against incremental pro-life bills
questions.answer: Silberman accepts that gaining ground matters but argues incremental bills aren't first downs at all; they're sacks and interceptions because they cede the foundational principle that all preborn humans deserve equal protection.
questions.quote: The hundreds of incremental bills passed in state legislatures do not amount to first downs. They amount to sacks and interceptions.

questions.question: How does Silberman challenge the claim that incremental laws are responsible for falling abortion rates?
questions.audience: state legislator or staffer weighing abolition bills against incremental pro-life bills
questions.answer: He notes that abortion rates are falling at similar rates in pro-life and pro-choice states and that Oklahoma's reported abortions have actually risen despite annual pro-life laws. He also points out that self-induced and chemical abortions go largely uncounted.
questions.quote: Here in Oklahoma, the number of abortions has actually [increased](https://lozierinstitute.org/abortion-reporting-oklahoma-2018/) in recent years despite the pro-life laws we pass every session.

questions.question: How does Elizabeth Heyrick's 1824 pamphlet support Silberman's case against gradualism?
questions.audience: abolitionist debater preparing rebuttals to common incrementalist arguments
questions.answer: Heyrick argued that the slave holder's smartest tactic was to coax abolitionists into demanding gradual rather than immediate emancipation, because the cry for gradualism breeds indifference and habituates the public to ongoing evil.
questions.quote: "The slave holder knew very well that his prey would be secure, so long as the abolitionists could be cajoled into a demand for gradual instead of immediate abolition. He knew very well, that the contemplation of a gradual emancipation, would beget a gradual indifference to emancipation itself."

questions.question: What is Barton's specific historical claim about John Quincy Adams in 1843, and why does Silberman doubt it?
questions.audience: history-minded reader fact-checking David Barton's claims about John Quincy Adams
questions.answer: Barton claims Adams introduced a bill in 1843 to abolish slavery over seven years and that abolitionists killed it, costing 22 more years of slavery and 600,000 Civil War deaths. Silberman finds no evidence such a bill existed, and notes Garrisonian immediatists had no significant congressional presence until the 1850s.
questions.quote: There does not appear to be any evidence that a bill to abolish slavery in the United States over seven years was introduced in 1843, and even if there was, the immediatist/Garrisonian abolitionists did not make significant inroads into Congress until years later in the 1850s and thus would not have had the ability to prevent an 1843 bill from passing even if it did exist.

questions.question: Which prominent American abolitionists is Barton implicitly accusing when he blames immediatists for prolonging slavery?
questions.audience: history-minded reader fact-checking David Barton's claims about John Quincy Adams
questions.answer: Silberman lists Garrison, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Tubman, David Walker, Sojourner Truth, Josiah Wedgewood, Elijah Lovejoy, Anthony Benezet, Arthur Tappan, Lewis Tappan, and Wendell Phillips as immediatists, meaning Barton's charge sweeps in nearly every famous American abolitionist.
questions.quote: Nearly all of the influential American abolitionists we read about – Garrison, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Tubman, David Walker, Sojourner Truth, Josiah Wedgewood, Elijah Lovejoy, Anthony Benezet, Arthur Tappan, Lewis Tappan, Wendell Phillips, etc. – were immediatists.

questions.question: Why does Silberman bring President John Tyler into the discussion of Barton's 1843 claim?
questions.audience: history-minded reader fact-checking David Barton's claims about John Quincy Adams
questions.answer: Tyler was president in 1843 and was a slave-owning white supremacist who later sat in the Confederate House of Representatives. Even if such an abolition bill had passed Congress, Tyler would almost certainly have vetoed it, undermining Barton's blame on abolitionists.
questions.quote: Needless to say, it is extraordinarily unlikely that Tyler ever would have signed a bill to abolish slavery over seven years even if the bill existed, making Barton's claim that the supposed bill "was not passed because [of] abolitionists" even more implausible.

questions.question: Who, according to Silberman, is actually preventing the diversified political strategy Barton invokes from Ecclesiastes 11?
questions.audience: state legislator or staffer weighing abolition bills against incremental pro-life bills
questions.answer: Incrementalist pro-life politicians and organizations are. The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act has been filed every year since 2016 and pro-life committee chairs, prompted by Oklahomans for Life, have refused even to give it a hearing.
questions.quote: The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act was first filed in 2016 and, every year, pro-life committee chairs have denied it even a committee hearing, [at the behest of Oklahomans for Life](https://theresurgent.com/2019/03/09/uncovered-letter-exposes-plan-to-keep-abortion-legal-in-oklahoma/).

questions.question: How does Silberman argue that incremental bills function as political cover for ostensibly pro-life politicians?
questions.audience: Texas Republican delegate or activist evaluating party platform priorities
questions.answer: He claims incremental bills give pro-life politicians a stockpile of 'victories' to campaign on while they actively block bills that would actually abolish abortion, allowing them to keep getting reelected even in the most pro-life states.
questions.quote: It's the incremental bills that these politicians use as a substitute for abolition that ultimately allows the politicians keeping abortion legal to get reelected in arguably the most pro-life state in the union.

questions.question: What does Silberman invite Barton to do at the close of the article?
questions.audience: pro-life Christian considering the immediatism vs. incrementalism debate
questions.answer: He invites Barton to a formal public debate on incrementalism versus immediatism and provides his email and Free the States contact form for follow-up.
questions.quote: I will extend to Barton – in the instance that this article reaches him or someone who knows him – the opportunity to formally debate the matter of incrementalism and immediatism.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Silberman explicitly cross-links to point #3 of this article when explaining how incremental bills can dehumanize the preborn; it expands the case sketched in problem #1.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Tagged 'Elizabeth Heyrick' and applies Heyrick's 1824 pamphlet (quoted at length here) directly to the abortion question.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Provides a concise framing of the same debate Silberman engages, useful as a primer for readers new to the categories.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Develops the theological objection that compromise bills cannot ground a prophetic witness, picking up the 'compromised foundations' argument from problem #1.

related.title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
related.reason: Applies the same critique to another high-profile evangelical defender of incrementalism, paralleling Silberman's reply to Barton.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Strengthens the biblical case against gradualist compromise bills that Silberman gestures at when invoking 'You shall not murder.'

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: A follow-up Free the States piece (one month later) showing how incremental pro-life laws fared in court, reinforcing Silberman's claim that they fail to change culture or law.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Abolitionists Rising's general statement of immediatist principles, useful background for the framework Silberman defends.
